prompt: How do I list available GGUF models for Kimari?
ideal: Kimari detects .gguf files in the configured models directory. Use: kimari models list to show detected models with name, size, and quantization. The models directory defaults to ~/.kimari/models/ but can be configured in kimari.yaml. Supported formats: GGUF v2 and v3 with various quantizations (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0, etc.).

prompt: What quantization should I use for a 7B model on 8GB VRAM?
ideal: For a 7B model on 8GB VRAM: Q4_K_M (4-bit, ~4.5GB) leaves room for context. Q5_K_M (~5.5GB) is better quality but less context. Q8_0 (~7.5GB) is near-lossless but very little context room. Recommendation: Q4_K_M for maximum context length, Q5_K_M for balanced quality/context. Never use Q2_K — quality degrades significantly. Measure actual memory with nvidia-smi during inference.

prompt: How do I run llama.cpp server with a GGUF model?
ideal: llama-server -m model.gguf --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080 -c 4096 -t 4. Key flags: --host for bind address (use 127.0.0.1 only for local), -c for context length, -t for threads, -ngl for GPU layers (--ngl 99 for all), -b for batch size. For GPU: add --ngl 99. Verify: curl http://127.0.0.1:8080/health. OpenAI-compatible endpoint: POST /v1/chat/completions.

prompt: What's the difference between GGUF and safetensors?
ideal: GGUF is a format optimized for inference with llama.cpp: single file, supports quantization, memory-mapped loading, includes metadata (tokenizer, chat template). Safetensors is a format for storing model weights: used by Hugging Face, faster to load for training, no quantization, separate tokenizer files. GGUF is for running models locally. Safetensors is for training and fine-tuning. Convert: python convert_hf_to_gguf.py model_dir --outtype Q4_K_M.

prompt: How do I convert a Hugging Face model to GGUF?
ideal: Use llama.cpp's conversion script: python convert_hf_to_gguf.py /path/to/model --outtype Q4_K_M --outfile model-Q4_K_M.gguf. Steps: 1) Clone/download model from HF. 2) Install llama.cpp with Python bindings. 3) Run convert script. 4) Optionally quantize: ./llama-quantize model-f16.gguf model-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M. The converted file includes tokenizer and metadata, making it self-contained for llama.cpp.

prompt: Can I run a 13B model on a GPU with 6GB VRAM?
ideal: Yes, with aggressive quantization. A 13B Q4_K_M model uses ~7.5GB which exceeds 6GB. Options: 1) Q3_K_M (~4.5GB) fits but with quality loss. 2) Q4_K_M with offloading: load some layers to GPU, rest to CPU (--gpu-layers 20). 3) Use a smaller model: 7B Q4_K_M (~4.5GB) fits comfortably. 4) Reduce context length (-c 2048). The GTX 1060 6GB can handle 7B Q4_K_M well, 13B requires compromises.

prompt: How do I benchmark inference speed of a GGUF model?
ideal: Use llama-bench from llama.cpp: ./llama-bench -m model.gguf -p 512 -n 128 -t 4. This measures prompt processing (tokens/s) and generation (tokens/s). Key metrics: prompt eval speed (higher is better for short inputs), generation speed (higher is better for longer outputs). Compare across quantizations to find the quality/speed sweet spot. Monitor GPU usage with nvidia-smi during the benchmark.

prompt: What is the context window and why does it matter for local LLMs?
ideal: Context window is the maximum number of tokens the model can process at once (input + output). Larger context = more conversation history and longer documents, but uses more memory. Memory scales roughly linearly: a 4K context uses ~2x more memory than 2K. For local models on 6-8GB VRAM: 2K-4K context is comfortable with Q4_K_M. 8K-16K requires more VRAM or GPU+CPU hybrid. Set context with -c flag in llama.cpp. Always benchmark your specific hardware.

prompt: How do I configure Kimari to use a specific GGUF model?
ideal: Edit kimari.yaml or use CLI: kimari config set model.path /path/to/model-Q4_K_M.gguf. Kimari automatically detects GGUF files in the models directory. Use kimari models list to see available models. Set default model: kimari config set model.default model-name. Context length: kimari config set model.context_length 4096. GPU layers: kimari config set model.gpu_layers 99 for full offload.

prompt: What should I do if my GGUF model produces garbage output?
ideal: Common causes: 1) Wrong chat template — ensure the model's template matches your prompt format. 2) Corrupted download — verify SHA256 checksum. 3) Wrong model type — some models need specific prompt formats (ChatML, Alpaca, etc.). 4) Too high temperature — try temp=0.1 for deterministic output. 5) Too low quantization — Q2_K produces poor results, use at least Q4_K_M. 6) Context overflow — reduce context length. Debug with: simple prompt, temperature 0, minimal context.

prompt: How do I run multiple models simultaneously on the same GPU?
ideal: It's generally not recommended — VRAM is limited and context switching is slow. Options: 1) Use llama-server with --parallel for batched inference of the same model. 2) Run on different GPUs if you have multiple. 3) Use CPU for one model and GPU for another (much slower). 4) Use a larger model instead of two small ones. 5) Use a model router that picks the right model per query (adds latency). Best approach: run one model at a time and switch as needed.

prompt: Explain the difference between GGUF quantization levels.
ideal: Q2_K: 2-bit, smallest size, worst quality. Q3_K: 3-bit, small, noticeable quality loss. Q4_K_M: 4-bit mixed, best balance of size/quality (recommended default). Q5_K_M: 5-bit mixed, better quality, ~25% larger than Q4. Q6_K: 6-bit, near-float quality, ~50% larger than Q4. Q8_0: 8-bit, virtually lossless, ~2x Q4 size. F16: full precision, ~4x Q4 size. Rule of thumb: Q4_K_M for 8GB+ VRAM, Q5_K_M for 12GB+, Q8_0 for 24GB+ or when quality is critical.

prompt: How do I check if my GPU supports the compute capability needed for llama.cpp?
ideal: Check with nvidia-smi: it shows GPU name and compute capability. llama.cpp needs compute capability 5.0+ (Maxwell and newer). For CUDA: GTX 900+ (5.0), GTX 1000+ (6.1), RTX 2000+ (7.5), RTX 3000+ (8.6), RTX 4000+ (8.9). Vulkan backend supports AMD and Intel GPUs. CPU-only mode works everywhere but is much slower. Use llama-cli --version to check your build's supported backends.

prompt: How do I create a custom system prompt for a GGUF model?
ideal: With llama-server: use the --system-prompt flag or include it in the chat template. With Kimari: kimari config set model.system_prompt 'You are a helpful assistant...'. With OpenAI-compatible API: include it as the system message in messages array. Best practices: be specific about role and constraints, include output format instructions, mention what the model should NOT do. Keep it under 500 tokens for best results.

prompt: What is KV cache and why does it matter for local inference?
ideal: KV cache stores the key-value pairs from attention computation so the model doesn't recompute them for each new token. Without KV cache, each new token requires reprocessing all previous tokens (O(n²)). With KV cache, generation is O(n) per token. KV cache uses VRAM proportional to: 2 * layers * hidden_dim * seq_len * batch_size. For a 7B model at 4K context: ~2GB of VRAM just for KV cache. This is why reducing context length frees significant memory.
